title: Preparing a lambing kit
subtitle: What to have ready before the first ewe goes into labour.
excerpt: Most lambing interventions are time-sensitive. A kit assembled and checked before lambing starts means you are not searching for iodine or a colostrum feeder at two in the morning when a ewe needs help.
sourceNotes: AHDB Beef and Lamb, BRP manual on lambing management. NADIS, lambing preparation factsheet. SAC Consulting, small flock lambing guide.

content.text: The lambing kit covers three things: the first hour after birth (navel care, colostrum), help if labour drags on (lubricant, clean sleeves), and the first 24 hours (fostering, warmth, feeding). Get everything ready at least two weeks before the first lambing date. Store it in a clean, dry place near the lambing area, easy to grab.

The navel cord is the primary infection route in a newborn lamb. Spray or dip the cord stump with iodine spray as soon as the lamb is on its feet, within the first 15 minutes of birth. Use a dedicated navel dip rather than a general antiseptic. Repeat once more at the next check. The cord will dry and drop off within a day or two; a wet, swollen, or smelly navel at 24 hours needs a vet.
Check that each lamb has suckled within the first two hours. A lamb that has suckled has a slightly rounded belly and will be lying resting rather than wandering and calling. Frozen ewe

from the previous season or commercial bovine colostrum are the fallbacks for a lamb that cannot suckle. Keep a supply of frozen colostrum from your own ewes; freeze it in 50 ml portions in zip-lock bags so you can defrost a small amount quickly. Thaw in warm water, not a microwave.

A ewe that has been in active labour for more than 30 to 45 minutes without a lamb appearing, or where a lamb is visible at the vulva but not progressing, needs assistance. Wash your hands and arms thoroughly, apply lambing lubricant generously, and check the lamb's position before any attempt to move it. If you cannot identify the problem or the lamb is in an abnormal position, call a vet rather than pulling. Never pull a lamb without first confirming its position.

Iodine navel spray or dip cup. Lambing lubricant and clean long-sleeved gloves. Stomach tube and syringe for feeding weak lambs unable to suckle. Clean towels for drying and warming lambs. Colostrum supply (frozen or commercial). A lamb coat or heat lamp for cold or weak lambs. Ear tag applicator and tags. Record book. Vet phone number on the wall.

Have at least one small pen (a bonding pen, roughly 1.5 m x 1.5 m) set up and bedded before lambing. Move each ewe and her lambs into a bonding pen immediately after lambing for 12 to 24 hours. Confined together in a small space, the ewe cannot walk away and the lambs learn her scent. Pairs can usually go back into the main group after 24 hours; triplets and difficult cases may need 48 hours. Preventing
is much easier than correcting it.
